segments.title: Harlech in Edward I's castle system
segments.summary: The opening places Harlech among Edward I's North Wales castles and explains the concentric design logic behind their walls, towers, and entrances.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins with a mid-twentieth-century castle guide to set Harlech inside the wider Edwardian fortification program in Wales. The reading explains high curtains, flanking towers, fortified entrances, and the concentric pattern in which lower outer works let defenders on the inner wall fire over them. This is a useful starting point for seeing Harlech as one example in a larger system of royal castles, towns, and engineered strongholds.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces Edward I's Welsh castle program and summarizes walls, towers, entrances, and fortified towns.
The transcript explains the concentric arrangement and compares North Wales castles with other British examples.
The reading links Edward I's fortification program to wider war, trade, town walls, and the shift toward defended houses.

segments.title: Harlech's lost maritime edge
segments.summary: The site discussion explains why modern Harlech is misleading: the castle was built on rock beside the sea, with a water gate and layered defenses.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains why Harlech puzzled him as a castle that was praised in guidebooks but not always foregrounded in plans. The modern landscape hides the original maritime setting, because the sea has retreated from the castle's water gate and rocky base. Seen as built, Harlech was a coastal fortress whose height, rock, gate access, and layered walls made sea supply and defense part of the same design problem.

evidence.note: The transcript explains the old guidebook, Harlech's appeal, and the later discovery of the castle image and plan puzzle.
The transcript describes the water gate, multiple outer works, rocky height, and strong defensive position.
The transcript explains that the sea has moved away from the castle and that Harlech was originally built straight to the sea.

segments.title: Edward's 1282 advance guard
segments.summary: The capture of the site is tied to Edward I's 1282 campaign and to a professional advance guard rather than a romantic picture of feudal warfare.
segments.body: The episode moves from geography into the campaign that brought Harlech under English control. Dr. Clarke describes Edward's movement through North Wales and the advanced party that took the site, stressing that the force was made up of hard professional soldiers rather than a picturesque host. That matters because the castle system was not just masonry; it rested on paid forces, military administration, and a wider campaign plan.

evidence.note: The transcript links Harlech to Edward's 1282 advance and the party that first took the site.
The transcript explains that the listed infantry were mostly professional archers with close-combat weapons and practical motivations.

segments.title: Construction, workforce, and cost
segments.summary: The construction story turns Harlech into a logistics project involving money, masons, quarrymen, carpenters, blacksmiths, laborers, and Master James of St George.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke tracks how quickly Edward I funded the initial work: money arrived in 1283, skilled workers followed, and the inner wall was already high enough for defense by winter. The later workforce figures make the site legible as a managed industrial project, not simply a romantic ruin. Harlech's cost, about ten percent of Edward's Welsh castle-building expenditure, is then used to argue that the king bought a durable strategic asset rather than a decorative monument.

evidence.note: The transcript describes rapid funding, arrival of carpenters and stonemasons, initial wall construction, a planned town, and Agnes as constable.
The transcript ties construction to Master James and lists the laborers, quarrymen, blacksmiths, carpenters, masons, and monthly cost.
The transcript gives the approximate total cost and compares Harlech's value with later fortification spending.

segments.title: Sea supply during siege
segments.summary: Harlech's wartime value is shown through later conflicts and the 1294 siege, when supplies reached the castle from Ireland by sea.
segments.body: This watch point is the clearest naval-history bridge in the episode. Dr. Clarke notes that Harlech took part in the Madog ap Llywelyn revolt, Owain Glyndwr's revolt, the Wars of the Roses, and the English Civil War, but the 1294 siege makes the logistics lesson especially plain. Fresh supplies came from Ireland by sea, showing why a castle built to the water could hold out in ways an inland stronghold could not.

evidence.note: The transcript lists later conflicts involving Harlech and argues that Edward got value for money.
The transcript gives the standard garrison and explains that supplies reached Harlech and Aberystwyth from Ireland by sea during the 1294 siege.
The transcript returns to the conquest of Wales and notes the Irish role in supplying food for the campaigns.

segments.title: Castles as royal psychology
segments.summary: The political section explains how castles worked as visible royal power for people who might never meet the king or hear reliable news about him.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke uses Edward I's personality, succession anxieties, and medieval expectations of rulership to explain why visible strength mattered. In a world where reputation moved through rumor rather than modern media, a maintained royal castle could become the local image of the king's authority. Harlech and the other coastal castles therefore worked as psychological warfare as well as military architecture.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Edward I's character, medieval rulership, heirs, public image, and reputation through word of mouth.
The transcript describes the castle chain, sea supply, local visibility, maintained garrisons, and the impression of royal strength.
The transcript explicitly frames castles as psychological warfare and contrasts appearance with actual defensive attributes.

segments.title: Geography, structure, and garrison
segments.summary: The closing assessment weighs Harlech by geography, structure, and garrison, showing why its sea edge and rock made it exceptional despite a small guard.
segments.body: The final analytical section gives viewers a simple framework for assessing castles: geography, structure, and garrison. Harlech scores strongly on its rock, height, sea access, gatehouse, moat, walls, and psychological presence, but Dr. Clarke still argues that a garrison of about thirty was too small. The closing image asks viewers to imagine the castle whitewashed, close to the water, and visibly representing Edward's power.

evidence.note: The transcript highlights the moat and gatehouse before introducing geography, structure, and garrison as assessment categories.
The transcript rates the garrison as weak and defines structural and geographic factors, including sea or river resupply and local population.
The transcript asks viewers to imagine Harlech with the sea at the water gate and the whitewashed castle projecting Edward's authority.
